column1: # text = Russia and Turkey: An 'alliance of misfits'?
# text = It was a gesture that ended a crisis.
# text = The leaders of Russia and Turkey met on Tuesday to shake hands and declare a formal end to an eight-month long war of words and economic sanctions.
# text = But, as Vladimir Putin greeted his Turkish counterpart in the gilded hall of a St. Petersburg palace, I got the distinct impression that Ankara wants this reconciliation the most.
# text = There was the handshake, yes.
# text = But Mr Putin's smile looked thin and he was hardly oozing warmth even by his own restrained standards.
# text = Recep Tayyip Erdogan by contrast talked repeatedly of his "dear friend" Mr Putin - five times, according to one report.
# text = He also pledged that relations with Russia would return not just to their pre-crisis level, but even higher.
# text = The next day one newspaper here described Mr Erdogan as acting as if nothing bad had ever happened.
# text = To me, his enthusiasm implied the opposite.
# text = But a lingering coolness emanating from Mr Putin showed that Russia's leader has forgotten nothing.
# text = In fact, the cause of the crisis was the first thing he mentioned in his opening comments:
# text = Turkey's shooting down of a Russian fighter plane on the Syrian border.
# text = Moscow's reaction at the time was furious.
# text = Mr Putin lashed out, accusing
# text = Ankara of stabbing Moscow in the back.
# text = The offence was even greater, coming from a supposed friend.
# text = Rebuilding real trust will be hard, perhaps impossible.
# text = Russian public opinion has also turned since November.
# text = For months, state-controlled media conducted a staggering, all-out offensive against Ankara.
# text = All of a sudden, it seemed like Turks were to blame for everything.
# text = Most serious were accusations from top officials that Mr Erdogan's own family has profited from an illegal trade in oil from areas of Syria controlled by the so-called Islamic State.
# text = But in St. Petersburg came the official message that it's time to move on.
# text = After all, this meeting only happened because Mr Putin got the apology he demanded from President Erdogan.
# text = Russia could claim a victory of sorts.
# text = For Ankara the benefits of calling a truce are clear.
# text = First and foremost, Erdogan needs all the friends he can get after he was nearly ousted from power last month in a failed coup.
# text = Repeat terror attacks on Turkey have clearly shaken him too.
# text = There is also an economic motive.
# text = Russian sanctions have hit hard - particularly the ban on charter flights, which usually carry several million Russian tourists to the Turkish coast each year.
# text = The number has slumped by almost 90%.
# text = As for Russia, tour operators and charter companies here will certainly be relieved when flights eventually resume.
# text = They're banking on a late-season rush to the Mediterranean.
# text = And even this week, state TV has been predicting cheaper fruit and vegetables once Turkish agricultural imports are permitted again.
# text = "Tourists that way, tomatoes back here," as a report in Vedemosti newspaper phrased it.
# text = But the visit also had additional political value for Moscow.
# text = Ankara is angry with the West for what it considers a weak response to the attempted takeover.
# text = Add to that its long-standing grudge at the snail's pace of talks to join the EU and step in Mr Putin - who is keen to capitalise on the chill and chip away at Turkey's ties with the West.
# text = The Russian leader certainly won bonus points with Ankara for calling in support of the elected authorities after the attempted coup.
# text = Mind you, that's a given for Moscow which has its own deep-seated fear of regime change.
# text = So the summit at this glitzy, seaside palace allowed Russia and Turkey to present what one analyst described to me as an "alliance of misfits":
# text = two countries that feel rejected and mistreated by the West, joining forces.
# text = Still, despite the public display of reconciliation, the two still have major differences.
# text = The key one is Syria, where Moscow has recently been casting itself as peacemaker but where Russia and Turkey back opposite sides.
# text = It could be telling that after almost three hours of initial talks, the two presidents told a press conference that they hadn't even touched on the topic.
# text = Turkey's president deliberately avoided answering a question on their differences, while Mr Putin chose to underline them.
# text = There is no clear consensus on where they can seek common ground on Syria.

# text = But after months of open hostility - and given the potential for utter disaster when Nato member Turkey shot down that Russian fighter jet - it is surely better that the two leaders are at least talking again.
